---
title: "B-mode systematics in<br>the UNIONS-3500 weak lensing survey"
occasion: CNRS AI Rising Talents — Interview
description: "Deep-dive: B-mode analysis, systematics characterisation, cosmological constraints"
author:
    name: Cail Daley
    affiliation: "CosmoStat, CEA Paris-Saclay  <br> <br> [on behalf of the UNIONS weak lensing team]{style=font-size:1.3em}"
date: June 25, 2026
date-format: long

format:
    revealjs:
        width:  1920
        height: 1080
        theme: [default, custom.scss]
        slide-number: true
        fig-align: center
        menu: false
        include-after-body: assets/no_footer_on_titleslide.html
        footer: "Cail Daley | CNRS AI Rising Talents | June 25, 2026"
        history: false
        hash: true
        pdf-separate-fragments: true
        pdf-max-pages-per-slide: 1
        template-partials:
            - title-slide.html
        center-title-slide: false
        logo: images/unions_wl_logo.png

html-math-method:
    method: mathjax
    url: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg-full.js
include-in-header: assets/mathjax.html
---

## Cosmic shear: what we measure {#shear-setup}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:60%"}

Gravitational lensing distorts galaxy shapes. We measure the **two-point shear correlations** — the statistical signal of the large-scale matter distribution.

::: {style="margin-top:0.6em"}
The shear field decomposes into:

- **E-modes** (curl-free): the cosmological lensing signal
- **B-modes** (divergence-free): zero from gravity alone
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.6em;font-style:italic"}
Non-zero B-modes diagnose residual systematics:
PSF leakage, additive shear bias, multiplicative calibration errors.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:40%"}

:::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![E-mode pattern](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/emode_pattern.jpg){width=100%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![B-mode pattern](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/bmode_pattern.jpg){width=100%}
:::
::::::

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
Establish the two-minute setup. The E/B decomposition is the key concept: E-modes are what we want, B-modes are what should be zero and tell us something is wrong when they aren't. The pattern images are intuitive.
:::


## UNIONS-3500 {#unions}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:55%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
- MegaCam/CFHT, **~3500 deg²**, $r$-band lensing
- Deepest wide-field weak lensing survey of the northern sky
- Gap-filler between KiDS and LSST: survey area + depth
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.8em"}
**Paper II is the gatekeeper.** It decides:

- which catalog version passes the null tests
- which angular scales are clean enough for cosmology

These choices are locked before unblinding.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:45%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/des_y6_footprints_unions.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
Brief. Key beats: northern sky (the gap), and Paper II as gatekeeper (establishes that this is methodologically principled — we're not fishing for a good S8 by choosing scales post-hoc).
:::


## Three statistics, one systematics test {#three-stats}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:58%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
We don't use one B-mode test — we use three, in different statistical bases:
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.6em"}
1. **$\xi_\pm^B$ (configuration space)** — pure E/B decomposition of the two-point correlation function; direct diagnostic

2. **COSEBIs** (Complete Orthogonal Sets of E/B-separating Integrals) — discrete modes maximally sensitive to B-mode contamination on specific scales

3. **$C_\ell^{BB}$ (harmonic space)** — power spectrum; different scale weighting than real-space statistics
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.6em;font-style:italic"}
Each weights angular scales differently. Requiring all three to pass is more conservative and more diagnostic than a single test.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:42%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/cl_data_vector.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
Two minutes here. Emphasise that the three bases are complementary: they weight scales differently, so disagreement between them carries information about *where* the contamination lives. This is the key setup for the next slide.
:::


## The B-mode data vector {#data-vector}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/pure_eb_data_vector.png){width=85% fig-align="center"}

::: notes
Let the figure breathe. Row by row: xi_+ E and B (top), xi_- E and B, COSEBIs E and B, Cl E and B. The B-mode rows should be consistent with zero — or not, as the case may be.
:::


## Not all statistics agree {#disagreement}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:52%"}

Over the **full angular range** (1–250 arcmin, $\ell \lesssim 2000$):

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
- $\xi_\pm^B$: **passes** ✓
- $C_\ell^{BB}$: **passes** ✓
- **COSEBIs: fails** ✗
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.8em"}
The disagreement is **not** real-space vs. harmonic-space — we compute COSEBIs from both $\xi_\pm$ and from $C_\ell$ bandpowers and they agree with each other.

The sensitivity is set by the **filter functions**, not the basis. COSEBIs' filters concentrate sensitivity on scales where there is contamination.
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.6em;font-size:0.9em;font-style:italic"}
The oscillating pattern is consistent with additive shear bias repeating at the CCD scale (Asgari et al. 2019). We cannot decisively identify the origin — this is an honest finding.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:48%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/cosebis_bsigma_talk.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
This is the key finding, ~2 min. The intuition: COSEBIs are designed to be maximally sensitive to specific B-mode patterns, so when they fail and the others pass, we've localised the contamination. The oscillating pattern in the figure is the signature.
The key intellectual move: "the statistics disagree, and that disagreement is information." It's not a failure of the analysis — it's the analysis working as designed.
:::


## Using disagreement to inform the analysis {#scale-cuts}

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
The B-mode tests are not a binary pass/fail. They determine:
:::

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:50%"}

**1. Catalog version selection**

Only the PSF size-corrected catalog passes across all statistics and scale cuts. This choice is locked before unblinding.

**2. Angular scale cuts**

COSEBIs pass after imposing:
- $\theta \geq 3.5'$ (for $\xi_+$)
- $\theta \geq 40'$ (for $\xi_-$)

These become the analysis scale cuts for Papers IV and V.

:::
::: {.column style="width:50%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/config_space_pte_fiducial.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
1.5 min. The point is that the disagreement does work: it selects the catalog version and sets the scale cuts. Both decisions are made before the cosmology is revealed.
:::


## Cosmological constraints {#cosmology}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:55%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
With validated catalog and frozen scale cuts, Papers IV (real space) and V (harmonic space) find:

- **S₈ = [value ± uncertainty]** — check paper for current blinded/unblinded value
- Agreement between two independent analyses (calibrated on 350 GLASS mocks)
- Agreement with Planck and Stage-III surveys at ~1σ
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.8em;font-size:0.9em"}
Dominant systematic: **intrinsic alignment prior** — S₈ is robust to most analysis choices but sensitive to the IA model. Marginalised conservatively.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:45%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/contour_survey_comparison.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
1.5 min. Lead with the headline number. The key point: two independent analyses (real space and harmonic space) agree. The IA prior dominance is an honest accounting — flag it as such.
:::


## Produced with agentic AI {#agentic}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:55%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
This analysis was conducted in agentic mode. Under my direction:

- ~10,000 lines of analysis code
- Three independent statistical frameworks
- ~120,000 lines edited by agents across the project
- Full manuscript (Daley et al. 2026)
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.7em"}
What this enables:

- Exploratory breadth impractical with hand-coding
- Every decision recorded in a structured provenance graph
- The verification question shifts: not "is the code correct" but "are the decisions correct" — where the astronomer's expertise belongs
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:45%"}

::: {style="margin-top:1.5em;text-align:center;font-size:0.85em"}
<!-- TODO: felt / ASTRA diagram showing decision graph structure -->
*[Decision provenance diagram — felt/ASTRA]*
:::

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
1 min. Not a detour — a forward pointer. This is how frontier cosmological analysis will be done in five years; we're already doing it.
The key beat: the verification question shifts. That's the deep point. It's not "use AI to go faster" — it's "use AI to make the breadth tractable while keeping the scientific judgement where it belongs."
:::


## Summary and forward {#summary}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:55%"}

**What we found**

- UNIONS-3500 delivers the first northern-sky cosmic shear constraints
- B-mode analysis: multiple statistics turn disagreement into diagnosis
- Validated S₈ constraints consistent with the concordance model

**What comes next**

- Tomographic cosmic shear (Year 1)
- Multi-probe analysis — shear + GGL + clustering (Year 3)
- The B-mode framework becomes the template for deeper UNIONS analyses

:::
::: {.column style="width:45%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/s8_convergence_with_unions.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: {style="margin-top:0.8em;font-style:italic;text-align:center"}
Thank you.
:::

::: notes
1 min. Same three-beat structure: what we found, what comes next. The figure shows UNIONS joining the S8 landscape — use it to close the loop on the northern sky gap you opened on the UNIONS slide.
:::
